title: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2025
short_title: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for
plain_summary: This bill updates a 1998 law to help the government better handle harmful algal blooms and low oxygen levels in water that can hurt people, animals, and the economy. It requires government agencies to watch for these water problems, predict when they might happen, and respond quickly when they do occur in oceans, lakes, and rivers. The bill also sets aside money for research into new ways to prevent and control these dangerous water conditions. It makes sure that Native American tribes and rural communities are included in planning and response efforts. The updated law will run through 2030 and expands which government agencies are involved in solving these water quality problems.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consider

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Amendments to the Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Act of 1998
sections.text: This section makes changes to an existing law from nineteen ninety eight about harmful algal blooms and hypoxia. The changes add the Department of Energy to a group of government agencies that work together on this issue. The changes also remove some old requirements and add new ones. The group must now create action plans every five years that look at harmful algal blooms in all types of water including the Great Lakes, rivers, lakes, and ocean waters. These plans must examine what causes these blooms, how they hurt wildlife and people, and what they cost communities. The plans must also look at ways to prevent or reduce these blooms and identify what research is needed. The group must include different regions in their plans to show how these blooms affect different areas. When creating these plans, the group must talk with state governments, Indian tribes, local governments, businesses like fishing and farming, universities, and other organizations that know about these issues.

sections.title: Section 2(b) National Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Program
sections.text: This part changes how the national program works. The program must now focus on monitoring, observing, and forecasting harmful algal blooms and hypoxia instead of just predicting them. The program must use both scientific assessments and action strategies to guide its work. It must work with programs that study marine, freshwater, and estuarine water systems instead of just oceans and Great Lakes. The program must examine what causes these blooms and how much they cost. It must also map areas with hypoxia each year in the Gulf of Mexico. The program must find ways to better monitor these problems, especially in waters that affect fishing, public health, or where people harvest food for survival. It must help the seafood industry deal with these blooms and make testing for harmful toxins more available, including in rural and remote areas. The program must provide real time information to help states, tribes, and local communities make decisions to protect health and local economies. It must also study how these blooms combine with other problems like runoff and changes to infrastructure to affect water systems and living resources.

sections.title: Section 2(c) National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Activities
sections.text: This part describes what the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration must do. The agency must respond to harmful algal bloom and hypoxia events in marine, coastal, and Great Lakes waters. It must develop and improve programs that observe and forecast these events, including monitoring, modeling, managing data, and sharing information. The agency must create forecast models that include how hurricanes and other weather events stir up nutrients in underwater sediments and how this affects harmful algal blooms. It must improve communication and coordination among federal agencies working on these issues. The agency must use resources and expertise from local research universities and institutions and use cost effective methods. All monitoring and observation data must follow standards set by the Integrated Coastal and Ocean Observation System Act of two thousand nine. This data must be made available through the National Integrated Coastal and Ocean Observation System.

sections.title: Section 2(d) Environmental Protection Agency Activities
sections.text: This part creates new requirements for the Environmental Protection Agency. The agency must research how freshwater harmful algal blooms and hypoxia events affect ecosystems and human health. It must develop and improve programs to monitor, observe, and forecast freshwater harmful algal blooms in lakes, rivers, and reservoirs. The agency must coordinate with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration on programs in the Great Lakes and estuaries. These programs must include monitoring, forecasting, modeling, managing data, and sharing information to support responding to events, setting priorities, prevention, adaptation, and reduction activities. The agency must improve communication and coordination among federal agencies doing freshwater harmful algal bloom and hypoxia work and research. It must use existing resources and expertise from federal and state partners and local research universities and institutions as much as possible. The agency must use cost effective methods and make sure its activities focus on new approaches that do not duplicate existing research programs.

sections.title: Section 2(e) National Harmful Algal Bloom Observing Network
sections.text: This part creates a national network to observe harmful algal blooms. The Under Secretary of Commerce, working through the National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science and the Integrated Ocean Observing System, must combine federal, state, regional, and local observation capabilities. This creates a national network of systems for monitoring, detecting, and forecasting harmful algal blooms. The network must use the capacity of regional associations of the Integrated Ocean Observing System and include new technologies and data integration methods. The Program Office of the Integrated Ocean Observing System must coordinate with the National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science about observations, data integration, and sharing information. It must organize, integrate, and share information to support ecological forecasting of harmful algal blooms. The office must also coordinate with the Water Quality Portal to store and serve data related to monitoring freshwater, estuarine, and coastal harmful algal blooms.

sections.title: Section 2(f) National Level Incubator Program
sections.text: This part creates a new national incubator program. The Under Secretary, working with the Environmental Protection Agency Administrator and research universities and institutions, must establish this program to increase the number of strategies, technologies, and measures available to prevent, reduce, and control harmful algal blooms. The program must create a framework for initial assessments of new strategies, technologies, and measures to determine how effective and scalable they could be. The program must provide merit based funding for strategies, technologies, and measures that eliminate or reduce harmful algae and associated toxins through biological, chemical, or physical means. The program must include a database that catalogs licensing and permitting requirements, economic costs, feasibility, effectiveness, and scalability of new and established strategies, technologies, and measures. When carrying out the program, the Under Secretary must prioritize proposed strategies, technologies, and measures that protect key fish and wildlife habitats, maintain biodiversity, protect public health, protect coastal resources of national, historical, and cultural significance, or benefit low income communities, Indian tribes, and rural communities.

sections.title: Section 2(g) Definitions
sections.text: This part updates the definitions used in the law. The Action Strategy means the action strategy for harmful algal blooms in the United States most recently submitted. A harmful algal bloom means a high concentration of marine or freshwater algae, large algae, or cyanobacteria that results in nuisance conditions or harmful impacts on marine and freshwater ecosystems, subsistence resources, communities, or human health. This can happen through the production of toxic compounds or other biological, chemical, or physical impacts of the bloom. A harmful algal bloom and hypoxia event means when a harmful algal bloom or hypoxia occurs due to natural, human caused, or unknown reasons. Hypoxia means a condition where dissolved oxygen in water is reduced to a level that threatens marine or freshwater organisms living in that system. Indian tribe has the same meaning as in the Indian Self Determination and Education Assistance Act. Native Hawaiian organization has the same meaning as in the Elementary and Secondary Education Act and includes the Department of Hawaiian Home Lands and the Office of Hawaiian Affairs. Subsistence use means the customary and traditional use of fish, wildlife, or other freshwater, coastal, or marine resources by any individual or community to meet personal or family needs, including essential economic, nutritional, or cultural applications. Tribal organization has the same meaning as in the Indian Self Determination and Education Assistance Act.

sections.title: Section 2(h) Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: This part sets the funding amounts for this program. For each year from two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty, Congress is authorized to provide nineteen million five hundred thousand dollars to the Under Secretary and eight million dollars to the Environmental Protection Agency Administrator to carry out this law. The Under Secretary or Administrator may transfer funds to the head of any federal department or agency, with that person's agreement, to carry out relevant parts of this law and another related law about drought information systems. This transfer authority must be specifically provided for in advance in appropriations acts.

sections.title: Section 3 Other Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Matters
sections.text: This section makes changes to another law about drought information systems from two thousand eighteen. The appropriate federal official may waive requirements for recipients to provide matching funds if the official determines there are no reasonable ways for the recipient to meet this requirement. The federal official may also enter into contracts, cooperative agreements, and grants with states, Indian tribes, tribal organizations, Native Hawaiian organizations, local governments, or other entities to pay for or reimburse costs they incur to determine and assess the environmental, economic, subsistence use, and public health effects of events of national significance. When deciding if a harmful algal bloom or hypoxia event is nationally significant, officials must consider leadership from affected Indian tribes, the District of Columbia, or affected territories or possessions of the United States. They must consider factors like risk to public health, severity of environmental effects, economic impacts, food safety and security impacts, subsistence impacts, and geographic scope. The section defines Indian tribe, Native Hawaiian organization, subsistence use, and tribal organization the same way as in the main law. Congress is authorized to provide two million dollars for each year from two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty to carry out this section, and this money remains available until spent.
